name: Weekly Breakout
checklist: 3M trend supports direction
1M trend supports direction
1W confirmation
Entry on daily trigger
Stop defined before entry

name: Expectancy & Asymmetry
description: How Matrix evaluates every setup — maximize long-term expectancy, not highest probability.
experimentHypothesis: Emphasizing maximum achievable asymmetry (vs waiting for stronger confirmation) improves long-term capital growth across a large statistical sample.
principles: Analysis Mode default — no JSON until explicit Apply intent
Matrix is a statistical engine, not a prediction engine
Maximize long-term expectancy — not highest probability
Maximize initial R — not win rate; 3R minimum target, 4R+ optimal when achievable
Entry Optimization: find structurally valid entries that maximize initial R
Prefer patience over premature entries; explain wait vs miss-move trade-offs
Layered entry improves average entry without changing the risk plan
Asymmetry over frequency — fewer high-R trades beat many low-R wins
Evaluate thesis quality AND opportunity quality — neither alone is sufficient
Confirmation has a cost — stronger thesis can mean weaker opportunity
One trade never validates or invalidates the Playbook
Experimental Control: during playbook validation, each qualified trade risks the same fixed capital amount — position size adjusts to stop distance so Entry Optimization remains the primary variable

methodology.philosophy: Matrix does not seek the highest probability trade. Matrix seeks the highest long-term expectancy. Every trade is one observation within a large statistical sample.
methodology.corePrinciple: Expected return = probability of success × reward relative to risk. Matrix evaluates whether the thesis is improving AND whether the remaining opportunity is still economically attractive.
methodology.asymmetryPrinciple: Maximum achievable R — not maximum theoretical R. Predefined risk, structural validity, realistic execution.
methodology.confirmationCost: Confirmation is not free. Stronger thesis can coexist with weaker investment opportunity. Evaluate Thesis Quality and Opportunity Quality separately.
methodology.opportunityPreservation: Delay only when expectancy measurably improves — not when certainty alone increases.
methodology.statisticalFramework: Losses are normal. Average winners must outweigh average losers. Long-term consistency over individual outcomes.
methodology.experimentalControl: During playbook validation, each qualified trade should risk the same fixed capital amount (e.g. USD 100). Wider stop → fewer shares; tighter stop → more shares. Same dollar cost if wrong — only entry quality and R vary. Otherwise you cannot tell whether results come from entry quality or bet size.
methodology.continuousLearning: Playbook evolution requires repeated evidence across multiple qualified trades — never one stock.
methodology.matrixIdentity: Matrix is a statistical engine, not a prediction engine. Every rule must improve long-term expectancy over a large sample — not make an individual trade feel more certain.

decisionPhilosophy: GO / WAIT / NO — maximize long-term expectancy, not certainty.

executionExperiments.strategyDefinition: Strategy determines whether a trade deserves capital (support entry, breakout, pullback, trend continuation). Strategy must remain constant during an execution experiment.
executionExperiments.executionDefinition: Execution determines how an approved trade is entered (single limit, layered limits, market). Execution may evolve independently of strategy.
executionExperiments.experimentalRule: Only one execution variable may be tested at a time. Everything else identical: thesis, stop, targets, fixed dollar risk per trade (shares adjust to stop distance), Stock File, Playbook.
executionExperiments.noChaseRule: If all predefined entry limits fail to execute, the trade is cancelled. Do not replace missed execution with a market order. A missed opportunity is preferable to breaking the experiment.
executionExperiments.executionPrinciple: Execution optimizes expectancy — average entry, R:R, capital efficiency. It must never rescue a poor thesis, justify chasing, or increase emotional discretion.
executionExperiments.layeredEntryHypothesis: Systematic layered limit placement improves average R:R without materially reducing participation — compared with single market or single limit entries.

checklist: Thesis quality scored independently from opportunity quality
Current R:R meets minimum
One execution variable only if running an execution experiment
No chase if all limits miss
Outcome = one statistical observation

statusDefinitions.active: Scout exists and is being monitored — entry zone not yet reached or decision pending.
statusDefinitions.filled: Predefined entry executed — scout converted to trade (full or partial fill per execution experiment).
statusDefinitions.missed: Predefined entry zone reached while thesis remained valid, but no execution occurred.
statusDefinitions.expired: Scout observation window ended without fill or explicit cancellation.
statusDefinitions.cancelled: Scout closed by user decision before fill — thesis may still be valid.

scoutStatistics.missedDefinition: Predefined entry zone reached while thesis remained valid, but no execution occurred.
scoutStatistics.notTradesRule: Missed Scouts remain part of Playbook statistics. They are NOT Trades. Do not count missed scouts as losses, wins, or closed trades.
scoutStatistics.purpose: Track opportunity cost and validate Entry Optimization experiments — measure how often valid setups pass without execution.

name: Layered Entry (Entry Optimization)
description: Improve average entry via predefined limit ladder — thesis, stop, targets, and size unchanged.
experimentHypothesis: Layered limit entries improve long-term expectancy vs single limit entries — measured by average entry improvement, fill rate, and net expectancy after 20–30 trades.
principles: Entry optimization — not scaling after confirmation
Thesis already accepted; improve average entry without changing risk plan
Fixed dollar risk per trade — shares adjust to stop distance (experimental control)
Capital 100% split across predefined limits (e.g. 40% / 35% / 25%)
No chase — highest limit missed = trade over
Experiment asks: does systematic limit placement improve R:R without killing participation?

executionExperiments.layeredEntryHypothesis: Can systematic limit placement improve average R:R without materially reducing participation?
executionExperiments.noChaseRule: Once the highest planned limit is missed, the trade is over. No market order. Emotion must not replace the plan.

checklist: Thesis approved — strategy locked
Fixed dollar risk per trade — shares sized to stop distance
Limit ladder defined with allocation % (sum = 100%)
Stop, targets, total dollar risk unchanged across experiment trades
No chase rule acknowledged
Record fill outcome: none / partial / full
Compare average entry vs first limit

name: Asymmetric Support Entry
description: Strategy: enter near structural support. Pair with layered-entry execution when optimizing fills.
principles: Strategy = support zone entry (constant during execution experiments)
Use layered-entry playbook for execution optimization
Setup stop ≠ thesis invalidation
Location evidence may justify GO before full confirmation — if opportunity quality holds
checklist: Stock File zone + minimum R:R defined
Thesis invalidation not breached
Execution method chosen (single_limit or layered_limits)
If layered: limits + allocations predefined, no-chase rule set

name: Multi-Timeframe Decision Hierarchy
description: Playbook experiment — top-down decision layers for the current ~3-month swing experiment. Hypothesis for analysis; not a mandatory engine rule.
experimentHypothesis: A strict A→B→C→D timeframe hierarchy reduces premature entries and clarifies when a Scout should exist — without lower frames invalidating the strategic thesis.
principles: Playbook experiment — may evolve; not hardcoded as engine rule
Top-down only: A before B before C before D
Lower timeframes cannot invalidate higher-timeframe thesis
Higher timeframes cannot justify buying regardless of price
Each timeframe answers a different question
If any grade fails, process stops — no trade proposed

multiTimeframeHierarchy.experimentNote: Playbook Experiment: Multi-Timeframe Decision Hierarchy — hypothesis for the current ~3-month swing experiment. Belongs in Playbook only; not duplicated in Stock Files.

grades.label: Opportunity
grades.question: Is there sufficient asymmetry?
grades.constraint: Decides whether a Scout should exist

grades.label: Tactical
grades.question: Has price reached the planned area?

multiTimeframeHierarchy.decisionRule: Top-down. A before B before C before D. If any grade fails, the process stops — no trade proposed.
importantRules: Lower timeframes cannot invalidate higher-timeframe thesis
Higher timeframes cannot justify buying regardless of price
Each timeframe answers a different question
This is a Playbook experiment and may evolve

checklist: Grade A — strategic thesis still valid (6M)
Grade B — sufficient asymmetry; scout zone defined (3M)
Grade C — price reached planned tactical area (1M)
Grade D — execution level, stop, entry refinement set (1W)
All grades pass top-down — otherwise stop, no trade

name: Structural Pullback Entry
description: Playbook experiment — compare battle zones by expected reach vs asymmetry before Entry Solver. Not a validated rule.
experimentHypothesis: In secular uptrends, waiting for structural pullback zones improves long-term expectancy vs buying momentum at extended prices.
principles: Playbook experiment — hypothesis to refute with data, not a permanent engine rule
Objective: where does the market offer greatest asymmetry — not where price will reverse
Zone Solver precedes Entry Solver — two separate problems
Battle zones are not automatic entries
Expected Reach Probability compares execution likelihood vs R quality — not prediction
Fibonacci and crowd-observed levels = reaction zones, not targets
Universe: secular uptrend, high liquidity, institutional-quality assets only
Reject hypothesis after 30–50 observations if pullbacks underperform momentum entries

zoneSelectionFlow.experimentNote: Zone Selection runs BEFORE Entry Solver. Entry Solver is unchanged — it resolves target, stop, maximum entry within the selected zone.
steps: 3. Rank zones by expected expectancy (reach × asymmetry)
4. Human or Scout selects preferred zone
zoneSelectionFlow.doesNotReplaceEntrySolver: Do not merge Zone Selection with Entry Solver. First decide WHERE battle is worth fighting; then solve HOW to enter.

structuralPullbackExperiment.objective: Determine whether waiting for structural pullbacks in secular uptrends produces higher long-term expectancy than participating during momentum.
structuralPullbackExperiment.hypothesis: Entering only after price reaches predefined structural retracement zones improves expectancy by increasing initial R while preserving sufficient participation.
universe: Higher highs and higher lows intact
Institutional-quality ETFs or large-cap leaders (e.g. VGT, VOO, SPY, QQQ, MSFT, AAPL)
structuralPullbackExperiment.expectedReachProbabilityNote: Not a prediction — compare balance between probability of execution and asymmetry quality (e.g. 118 high reach/low R vs 80 low reach/excellent R).
entryProcess: Apply Entry Solver: probable target, strategy stop, maximum entry, minimum R
Execute only if all Playbook rules satisfied
structuralPullbackExperiment.corePrinciple: Identify where the market offers the greatest asymmetry relative to risk — not where price will reverse.
structuralPullbackExperiment.guidingPrinciple: Maximize expected value per unit of risk consumed — not participation rate.
structuralPullbackExperiment.zoneSolverNote: Zone Solver compares candidate zones (reach probability × asymmetry). Only the selected zone receives Entry Solver and Scout Plan.
battleZoneRules: Zones do not create trades automatically
One zone selected → one Scout Plan
Zones can be invalidated without deleting thesis
layeredEntryRules: Single strategy stop for entire plan
Fixed total dollar risk — shares per layer from stop distance
All layers must meet minimum R
If no limit fills, trade cancelled — no market chase

checklist: Secular thesis valid — no structural invalidation
Battle zones identified and ranked (reach × asymmetry)
Preferred zone selected — not buying at extended momentum by default
Entry Solver applied only inside selected zone
Layered limits + fixed dollar risk if using execution experiment
No chase if all limits miss
Outcome feeds Structural Pullback experiment metrics
